mission.title: Install and Configure Capsule on Kubernetes
mission.description: Capsule is a multi-tenancy and policy-based framework for Kubernetes that simplifies the management of multiple namespaces within a single cluster. By using Capsule, you can efficiently group namespaces into tenants and enforce policies, making it easier to manage resources and security across your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Capsule Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Capsule Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration. This allows you to install the Capsule Operator using Helm.
```bash
helm repo add capsule https://ghcr.io/projectcapsule/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Capsule Operator
steps.description:
Next, install the Capsule Operator in a dedicated namespace. This command will create the namespace if it doesn't already exist.
```bash
helm install capsule capsule/capsule --namespace capsule-system --create-namespace --version 0.12.4
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Capsule Operator pod is running successfully. This command will list all pods in the capsule-system namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace capsule-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for the Capsule Operator
steps.description:
To ensure that the Capsule Operator has appropriate resource limits, you can edit the values.yaml file or pass resource limits directly during installation. Here’s an example of how to set resource limits:
```bash
helm upgrade capsule capsule/capsule --namespace capsule-system --set global.jobs.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set global.jobs.resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for Capsule
steps.description:
Ensure that the necessary Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) permissions are in place for the Capsule Operator. You can apply the following RBAC configuration:
```bash
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: capsule-role
rules:
- apiGroups: ['']
  resources: ['pods', 'pods/log']
  verbs: ['get', 'list', 'watch']
EOF
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Capsule will result in the Capsule Operator running in the 'capsule-system' namespace, with its pods in a 'Running' state. You should also have resource limits configured and RBAC permissions set up for the operator.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add capsule https://ghcr.io/projectcapsule/charts
helm repo update
helm upgrade capsule capsule/capsule --namespace capsule-system --set global.jobs.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set global.jobs.resources.limits.memory=512Mi

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: capsule-role
rules:
- apiGroups: ['']
  resources: ['pods', 'pods/log']
  verbs: ['get', 'list', 'watch']
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and that Helm and kubectl are installed on your local machine.
